216	\begin{lemma}
217	\leanok
218	[Dense-open agreement: the geometric heart of the Rigidity Lemma]
219	  \label{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
220	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
221	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
222	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let \(X\) be a complete variety, \(Y\) and \(Z\)
223	  % any varieties, and \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) a morphism such that for some
224	  % \(y_0 \in Y\), \(f(X \times \{y_0\})\) is a single point \(z_0\) of \(Z\). Then there is a
225	  % morphism \(g \colon Y \to Z\) such that if \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is the
226	  % projection, \(f = g \circ p_2\)."
227	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43 (the
228	  dense-open construction is the body of that proof).}
229	  Let \(X\) be a complete variety and \(Y\), \(Z\) any varieties over \(\bar k\). Let
230	  \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) be a morphism, fix a point \(x_0 \in X\), and suppose given
231	  \(y_0 \in Y\), \(z_0 \in Z\) with the \emph{collapse hypothesis}
232	  \[ f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \qquad
233	     \text{(in Lean: } \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathbf 1_X)\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi y_0) \fatsemi f
234	     = \mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi z_0\text{).} \]
235	  Then there is a non-empty open subset \(U \subseteq X \times Y\) on which \(f\) agrees, as a
236	  scheme morphism, with the collapsed map \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\), where
237	  $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,
238	  (p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y)\( is the ``\)(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$'' endomorphism. Concretely
239	  \(U = X \times V\) with \(V := Y - G\), \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(Z - U_0))\) for an affine open
240	  neighbourhood \(U_0\) of \(z_0\), and on \(U\) one has \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y)\).
241	  \medskip
242	  \noindent\textbf{The collapse hypothesis is load-bearing.} The non-emptiness of \(V\) is exactly
243	  the assertion \(y_0 \notin G\), and this holds \emph{because} $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}
244	  \subseteq U_0\( keeps the slice over \)y_0\( inside the affine \)U_0\(, away from \)F = Z - U_0$. A
245	  version of this lemma \emph{without} the collapse hypothesis is false: for \(X = Y = Z\) and
246	  \(f = p_1\) the first projection, \(f\) and \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f \colon (x, y) \mapsto x_0\)
247	  agree on no non-empty open (the agreement locus is contained in \(\{x = x_0\}\), which has empty
248	  interior in the irreducible \(X \times Y\)). Therefore the formal target \emph{must} carry the
249	  collapse hypothesis as an explicit antecedent; dropping it (as an earlier decomposition
250	  silently did) makes the deferred goal unsatisfiable.
251	  \medskip
252	  \noindent\textbf{Formalization note: algebraically closed base, and locally-of-finite-type
253	  source.} The prose already takes \(\bar k\) algebraically closed, but the formal statement must
254	  \emph{encode} two properties. First, the cohomology-free proof of the slice-constancy bridge
255	  (below) needs each per-closed-point residue field \(\kappa(y)\) to be \(\bar k\) itself, so that a
256	  proper integral slice has global sections \(\Gamma = \bar k\) and maps to a single \(\bar k\)-point of
257	  the affine \(U_0\); this requires \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\). Second, the globalisation of the
258	  per-slice agreement (Step~2 of the bridge, \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}) needs the
259	  closed points of the saturated open \(U\) to be \emph{dense} --- i.e.\ \(U\) to be a Jacobson space.
260	  Over the algebraically closed \(\bar k\) this holds precisely because \(X \times Y\) is \emph{locally of
261	  finite type} over \(\bar k\) (\texttt{LocallyOfFiniteType.jacobsonSpace} transports the Jacobson
262	  property of \(\Spec \bar k\), and \texttt{closure\_closedPoints} then gives density); this requires
263	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\). Accordingly the formalization carries
264	  \emph{both} \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\) and
265	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\) on this lemma (in addition to
266	  \([\mathtt{Field}\ \bar k]\)), and propagates the same two instances to \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma},
267	  its core helper \texttt{rigidity\_core}, and the bridge sub-lemmas
268	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} and
269	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}. Both cost nothing downstream: the
270	  consumers \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} and \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} (Lean
271	  \texttt{rigidity\_genus0\_curve\_to\_grpScheme}) \emph{already} assume
272	  \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\) --- the base-field variable is literally named \(\mathtt{kbar}\) ---
273	  and curves and abelian varieties are of finite type over \(\bar k\), so the
274	  locally-of-finite-type instance is automatic there.
275	\end{lemma}
276	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Choose any point \(x_0 \in X\), and define \(g \colon Y \to Z\) by
277	% \(g(y) = f(x_0, y)\). Since \(X \times Y\) is a variety, to show that \(f = g \circ p_2\), it is
278	% sufficient to show that these morphisms coincide on some open subset of \(X \times Y\). Let
279	% \(U\) be an affine open neighbourhood of \(z_0\) in \(Z\), \(F = Z - U\), and \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))\);
280	% then \(G\) is closed in \(Y\) since \(X\) is complete and hence \(p_2\) is a closed map. Further
281	% \(y_0 \notin G\) since \(f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}\). Therefore \(Y - G = V\) is a non-empty
282	% open subset of \(Y\). For each \(y \in V\), the complete variety \(X \times \{y\}\) gets mapped by
283	% \(f\) into the affine variety \(U\), and hence to a single point of \(U\). But this means that for
284	% any \(x \in X\), \(y \in V\), \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)\), and this proves our
285	% assertion."
286	\begin{proof}
287	  \leanok
288	  Let \(U_0\) be an affine open neighbourhood of \(z_0\) in \(Z\), put \(F = Z - U_0\), and set
289	  \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))\). Because \(X\) is complete, the projection \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is a
290	  \emph{closed} map, so \(G\) (the image of the closed set \(f^{-1}(F)\)) is closed in \(Y\). The
291	  collapse hypothesis \(f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U_0\) gives
292	  \(f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing\), hence \(y_0 \notin G\); this is the only place
293	  the collapse hypothesis is used, and it is essential, for \(V := Y - G\) is non-empty precisely
294	  because \(y_0 \in V\). For each \(y \in V\) the proper variety \(X \times \{y\}\) is mapped by \(f\)
295	  into the affine \(U_0\), hence to a single point, necessarily \(f(x_0, y)\). So for all \(x \in X\),
296	  \(y \in V\), we have \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x, y)\), i.e.\ \(f\) and
297	  \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree on the non-empty open \(U := X \times V\). (In the Lean
298	  encoding the two slice-constancy bridges are the closed-map step from properness and the
299	  ``proper-into-affine has single-point image'' step; see the docstring of
300	  \texttt{rigidity\_core}.)
301	  \medskip
302	  \noindent\textbf{Formalization notes (the concrete char-free Mathlib route).} The textbook
303	  proof above is realised in Lean by three pieces. They cite Mathlib lemma names as the chosen
304	  route; none uses cohomology, and the relative Stein-factorisation framing is deliberately
305	  \emph{avoided} (see below).
306	  \emph{Bridge~1 (the closed-map step) --- BUILT.} ``\(X\) complete \(\Rightarrow p_2\) closed'' is
307	  the axiom-clean helper \texttt{snd\_left\_isClosedMap} (iter-158). It is obtained from
308	  \texttt{IsProper.toUniversallyClosed} on \(X\), base-changed across the canonical pullback square
309	  to make \(p_2\) universally closed, using that the monoidal projection \emph{is} the chosen
310	  pullback projection: \texttt{Over.snd\_left} rewrites \((\mathtt{snd}\,X\,Y).\mathtt{left}\) to
311	  \(\mathtt{Limits.pullback.snd}\ X.\mathtt{hom}\ Y.\mathtt{hom}\) exactly. This step is already
312	  proven.
313	  \emph{The fibre fact (non-emptiness of \(V\)) --- RESOLVED route, char-free.} That the slice
314	  \(X \times \{y_0\}\) over the \(\bar k\)-rational point \(y_0\) is exactly the image of the slice
315	  section \(s \colon X \to X \times Y\) is formalised \emph{without} the residue-field /
316	  \texttt{Triplet} / tensor-product machinery (that fine \texttt{carrierEquiv} layer is the wrong
317	  granularity and is to be avoided). One pastes the identity square for the section against the
318	  canonical pullback square (\texttt{IsPullback.of\_right},
319	  \texttt{IsPullback.of\_horiz\_isIso}; the section identities \(s \fatsemi p_1 = \mathbf 1\) and
320	  \(s \fatsemi p_2 = X.\mathtt{hom} \fatsemi y_0\) come from \texttt{lift\_fst}/\texttt{lift\_snd}
321	  with \texttt{Over.w} on the unit) and reads off the fibre with the \emph{coarse} topological
322	  layer of \texttt{PullbackCarrier}, namely
323	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pullback.image\_preimage\_eq\_of\_isPullback}. This needs no
324	  \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}]\).
325	  \emph{Bridge~2 (slice-constancy / the agreement equation) --- RESOLVED route, cohomology-FREE.}
326	  ``Proper connected slice into affine \(\Rightarrow\) single point, glued to a morphism equality on
327	  \(U\)'' is realised by a \textbf{global-sections + per-closed-point} route, \emph{not} by any
328	  relative Stein-factorisation or proper-pushforward \(f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O\) statement: the
329	  latter is a genuine Mathlib gap (no Stein factorisation, no \(H^0\) base change), and route~(c)
330	  exists precisely to avoid coherent cohomology, so it must \emph{not} be attempted. The concrete
331	  stack is: for each closed point \(y \in V\) one has \(\kappa(y) = \bar k\) (alg-closed, finite type);
332	  the slice \(X_y\) is proper integral, so its global sections form a field
333	  (\texttt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}) module-finite over \(\bar k\)
334	  (\texttt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}), and algebraic closedness kills the finite
335	  extension to give \(\Gamma(X_y) = \bar k\). A morphism into the affine \(U_0\) is pinned by its ring
336	  map on global sections (\texttt{ext\_of\_isAffine}), so \(f\) and
337	  \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree on each closed slice. Closed points are dense in the
338	  locally-of-finite-type \(U\) (\texttt{closure\_closedPoints} / the Jacobson-space property of
339	  locally-of-finite-type \(\bar k\)-schemes; in the formalization this density is supplied by the new
340	  hypothesis \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\)), so the per-slice
341	  agreement globalises to all of \(U\) via the reduced-source / separated-target rigidity
342	  \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (the same handle \texttt{rigidity\_core} already
343	  uses). The packaged ``morphisms agreeing on a dense set of closed points are equal''
344	  hom-extensionality --- the one connective Mathlib does not supply directly (it gives only the
345	  single-dominant-morphism \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant}) --- has now been built as the axiom-clean
346	  \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints} (Step~2): it assembles the closed points into one
347	  dominant coproduct probe and applies \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant}. The whole argument is extracted
348	  into the dedicated obligation \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} below, whose body
349	  wires that proven Step~2 over the per-slice Step~1
350	  (\cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}); Step~1 (Mumford's per-closed-slice
351	  constancy) is now the chain's \emph{single} genuinely-deep residual \texttt{sorry}.
352	\end{proof}
353	\begin{lemma}
354	\leanok
355	[Bridge~2: slice-constancy on a saturated open mapping into an affine]
356	  \label{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine}
357	  
358	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine}
359	  % NOTE (iter-161): the iter-160 SIGNATURE GAP is RESOLVED. The Lean proof wires Step 2
360	  % (morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints, PROVEN axiom-clean) over Step 1
361	  % (rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine, the residual sorry). Step 2 needs the closed
362	  % points of U to be DENSE, i.e. U Jacobson; over k̄ this follows from (X⊗Y) being locally of finite
363	  % type, so [LocallyOfFiniteType (X⊗Y).hom] is now a stated hypothesis of this lemma (and across the
364	  % chain). With it, the `JacobsonSpace U` instance is a routine discharge (LocallyOfFiniteType.
365	  % jacobsonSpace + JacobsonSpace.of_isOpenEmbedding), no longer an as-typed gap. The only `sorry`
366	  % reachable from this lemma is now Step 1 (Mumford's per-closed-slice constancy). The hypothesis
367	  % set is recorded in the statement and proof prose below (both [IsAlgClosed k̄] AND
368	  % [LocallyOfFiniteType (X⊗Y).hom]); the earlier "[IsAlgClosed] is the only added instance" claim
369	  % is retired.
370	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
371	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "For each \(y \in V\), the complete variety \(X \times \{y\}\) gets mapped by
372	  % \(f\) into the affine variety \(U\), and hence to a single point of \(U\). But this means that for
373	  % any \(x \in X\), \(y \in V\), \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)\), and this proves our
374	  % assertion." [Mumford, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma, p.~43.]
375	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43 (the
376	  ``for each \(y \in V\), the complete slice maps into the affine, hence to a single point'' step).}
377	  Let \(\bar k\) be algebraically closed and let \(X\), \(Y\), \(Z\) be objects of
378	  \(\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)\) with \(X\) proper, \(X \times Y\) geometrically irreducible, reduced,
379	  and \emph{locally of finite type} over \(\bar k\) (the formalization carries
380	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\), which makes the saturated open \(U\)
381	  below a Jacobson space, so its closed points are dense --- the hypothesis Step~2 of the proof
382	  consumes), and \(Z\) separated. Let \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) be a morphism, fix a \(\bar k\)-point
383	  \(x_0 \colon \mathbf 1 \to X\), and write $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,
384	  (\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,(p_2)\( for the ``\)(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$''
385	  endomorphism. Suppose:
386	  \begin{itemize}
387	    \item \(U \subseteq X \times Y\) is a \(p_2\)-\emph{saturated} open, i.e.\ \(U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})\)
388	      for a set \(V_{\mathrm{set}} \subseteq Y\) (so \(U\) contains the whole fibre \(X_y\) over each of
389	      its points);
390	    \item \(U_0 \subseteq Z\) is an \emph{affine} open;
391	    \item \(f(U) \subseteq U_0\), i.e.\ \(f\) maps \(U\) into the affine \(U_0\).
392	  \end{itemize}
393	  Then \(f\) and \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree on \(U\) as scheme morphisms:
394	  \(U.\iota \fatsemi f = U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)\).
395	\end{lemma}
396	\begin{proof}
397	  \leanok
398	  This is the formalization realization of Mumford's step ``for each \(y \in V\), the complete
399	  variety \(X \times \{y\}\) gets mapped by \(f\) into the affine variety \(U\), and hence to a single
400	  point of \(U\)''. We realise it by a \textbf{global-sections + per-closed-point} route that is
401	  \emph{cohomology-free}; in particular the relative Stein-factorisation / proper-pushforward
402	  \(f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O\) framing is a confirmed Mathlib gap (no Stein factorisation, no
403	  \(H^0\) base change) and is \emph{deliberately avoided}. The argument splits into two named
404	  sub-lemmas, one residual and one proven.
405	  \medskip
406	  \noindent\textbf{Step 1 (per-closed-slice constancy) --- \cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}, the residual.}
407	  Fix a closed point \(x\) of \(U\), lying over a closed point \(y = p_2(x) \in V_{\mathrm{set}}\).
408	  Since \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed and \(Y\) is of finite type over \(\bar k\), the residue field
409	  is \(\kappa(y) = \bar k\). Because \(U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})\) is saturated, \(U\) contains the
410	  \emph{entire} fibre \(X_y \cong X\) over \(y\); hence \(f\) maps the proper integral slice \(X_y\) into the
411	  affine \(U_0\). By \(\mathtt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}\) the global sections \(\Gamma(X_y)\) form a
412	  field, module-finite over \(\bar k\) by \(\mathtt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}\), and
413	  algebraic closedness collapses the finite extension to give \(\Gamma(X_y) = \bar k\). A morphism into
414	  the affine \(U_0\) is pinned by its ring map on global sections (\(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}\)), so the
415	  restriction of \(f\) to \(X_y\) factors through a single \(\bar k\)-point of \(U_0\) --- necessarily the
416	  point \(f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x)\). Thus \(f\) and \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\)
417	  agree (after the residue-field probe) at every closed point of \(U\). This per-slice statement is the
418	  chain's single genuinely-deep residual \texttt{sorry}.
419	  \medskip
420	  \noindent\textbf{Step 2 (globalisation over dense closed points) --- \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}, proven.}
421	  The closed points are dense in the locally-of-finite-type \(\bar k\)-scheme \(U\)
422	  (\(\mathtt{closure\_closedPoints}\) / the Jacobson-space property, supplied here by the new hypothesis
423	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\)). To turn ``\(f\) and
424	  \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree at each closed point'' into a single morphism equality on \(U\),
425	  the proven globalisation \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints} assembles the closed points
426	  into one dominant probe --- the coproduct
427	  \(\coprod_{x \in \mathrm{closedPoints}\,U} \Spec \kappa(x) \to U\), whose range is dense --- and feeds
428	  it to the reduced-source / separated-target rigidity handle \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\) (the same
429	  family \(\mathtt{rigidity\_core}\) uses). This yields the morphism equality
430	  \(U.\iota \fatsemi f = U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)\) on all of \(U\). The
431	  ``agrees on a dense set of closed points \(\Rightarrow\) hom-equality'' connective is the one piece
432	  Mathlib does not package directly (it supplies only the single-dominant-morphism
433	  \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\)); that bespoke build is now done in
434	  \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}, and it is \emph{not} cohomology. \qedhere
435	\end{proof}
436	\begin{lemma}\leanok
437	[Step~2: morphisms agreeing at every closed point of a Jacobson source are equal]
438	  \label{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}
439	  
440	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}
441	  % Project-bespoke assembly: NO external source. Mathlib supplies only the single-dominant-morphism
442	  % ext_of_isDominant; the "dense closed points => hom-extensionality" connective is built here.
443	  Let \(W\), \(Z\) be schemes with \(W\) \emph{reduced}, \(W\) a \emph{Jacobson space}, and \(Z\)
444	  \emph{separated}. Let \(g_1, g_2 \colon W \to Z\) be morphisms. If, for every \emph{closed} point
445	  \(x \in W\), the two morphisms agree after restriction along the residue-field point
446	  \(\Spec \kappa(x) \to W\) (in Lean, $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_1 =
447	  W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_2\(), then \)g_1 = g_2$.
448	\end{lemma}
449	\begin{proof}
450	\leanok
451	  This is the ``dense closed points \(\Rightarrow\) hom-extensionality'' connective that Mathlib does
452	  not package directly: it supplies only the single-dominant-morphism extensionality
453	  \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\) (a dominant morphism into a separated target is an epimorphism for
454	  the purpose of right-cancellation). We assemble the per-closed-point hypotheses into one such
455	  dominant probe.
456	  Form the coproduct \(P := \coprod_{x \in \mathrm{closedPoints}\,W} \Spec \kappa(x)\) and the morphism
457	  \(\mathrm{probe} \colon P \to W\) defined by \(\mathtt{Sigma.desc}\) of the residue-field points
458	  \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x\). Its topological range contains every closed point of \(W\):
459	  the inclusion \(\iota_x \colon \Spec \kappa(x) \to P\) composes with \(\mathrm{probe}\) to
460	  \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x\) (the defining property of \(\mathtt{Sigma.desc}\)), and the
461	  range of \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x\) is the singleton \(\{x\}\)
462	  (\(\mathtt{Scheme.range\_fromSpecResidueField}\)). Because \(W\) is a Jacobson space, the closed points
463	  are dense (\(\mathtt{closure\_closedPoints}\), i.e.\ \(\overline{\mathrm{closedPoints}\,W} = W\)), so
464	  the range of \(\mathrm{probe}\) is dense and \(\mathrm{probe}\) is \emph{dominant}.
465	  Componentwise, \(g_1\) and \(g_2\) agree after \(\mathrm{probe}\): precomposing with each coproduct
466	  inclusion \(\iota_x\) reduces (by \(\mathtt{Sigma.\iota\_desc}\)) to
467	  \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_1 = W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_2\),
468	  which is the hypothesis at the closed point \(x\). By the universal property of the coproduct
469	  (\(\mathtt{Sigma.hom\_ext}\)) this gives \(\mathrm{probe} \fatsemi g_1 = \mathrm{probe} \fatsemi g_2\).
470	  Since \(\mathrm{probe}\) is dominant and \(Z\) is separated, \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\) cancels it
471	  on the left to yield \(g_1 = g_2\).
472	\end{proof}
473	\begin{lemma}
474	\leanok
475	[Global-sections constancy: two \(\bar k\)-points of a proper integral source into an affine agree]
476	  \label{lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}
477	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}
478	  % Project-bespoke assembly of Mathlib lemmas: NO external source. Realises, cohomology-free, the
479	  % classical "a global regular function on a proper integral variety over an algebraically closed
480	  % field is constant" --- precisely the affine-image step of Mumford's Rigidity Lemma proof quoted
481	  % above (the "complete slice into an affine maps to a single point" sentence, Ch.~II \S4, p.~43).
482	  Let \(\bar k\) be algebraically closed and let \(W\) be an integral scheme equipped with a structure
483	  morphism \(w \colon W \to \Spec \bar k\) that is universally closed (proper) and locally of finite
484	  type. Let \(g \colon W \to V\) be a morphism into an \emph{affine} scheme \(V\). Then any two
485	  \(\bar k\)-points of \(W\) --- i.e.\ any two sections \(a, b \colon \Spec \bar k \to W\) of \(w\) (so
486	  \(a \fatsemi w = \mathbf 1\) and \(b \fatsemi w = \mathbf 1\)) --- satisfy \(a \fatsemi g = b \fatsemi g\).
487	  \medskip
488	  \noindent\textbf{Every hypothesis is load-bearing} (confirmed by the iter-161 audit).
489	  \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\) collapses the finite extension \(\Gamma(W)/\bar k\); integrality of
490	  \(W\) makes \(\Gamma(W)\) a domain (without it a two-point disjoint union is a counterexample);
491	  \(\mathtt{UniversallyClosed}\) is what upgrades \(\Gamma(W)\) to a \emph{field} (without it
492	  \(\mathbb A^1\), with \(\Gamma = \bar k[t]\) and two distinct \(\bar k\)-points, is a counterexample);
493	  \(\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\) gives finiteness of \(\Gamma(W)\) over \(\bar k\); and
494	  \([\mathtt{IsAffine}\ V]\) is what lets \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}\) pin the morphism by its ring map
495	  on global sections.
496	\end{lemma}
497	\begin{proof}
498	\leanok
499	  Because \(W\) is integral and \(w\) is universally closed, the global sections
500	  \(\Gamma(W, \mathcal O_W)\) form a field (\(\mathtt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}\)), and the structure
501	  ring map \(w^\sharp \colon \bar k \to \Gamma(W)\) (the map \(\mathtt{wk.appTop}\) after the canonical
502	  identification \(\Gamma(\Spec \bar k) \cong \bar k\)) is integral / module-finite
503	  (\(\mathtt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}\)). Since \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed and
504	  \(\Gamma(W)\) is a domain finite over it, that map is bijective
505	  (\(\mathtt{IsAlgClosed.ringHom\_bijective\_of\_isIntegral}\)); thus \(\Gamma(W) = \bar k\) and
506	  \(w^\sharp\) is an isomorphism on global sections --- in particular an epimorphism. Both sections
507	  \(a\), \(b\) left-invert \(w^\sharp\) on global sections (they are sections of \(w\)), so they induce the
508	  \emph{same} ring map \(a^\sharp = b^\sharp\) on \(\Gamma(W)\) (cancel the epimorphism \(w^\sharp\)). A
509	  morphism into the \emph{affine} \(V\) is pinned by its global-sections ring map
510	  (\(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}\)), whence \(a \fatsemi g = b \fatsemi g\). This realises, without
511	  cohomology, the classical ``proper connected variety into an affine is a single point'' step
512	  quoted from Mumford above.
513	\end{proof}
514	\begin{lemma}
515	\leanok
516	[Integrality descends to a retract of an integral scheme]
517	  \label{lem:isIntegral_of_retract_of_integral}
518	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isIntegral_of_retract}
519	  % NOTE: (iter-162 review) project-bespoke (elementary topology + algebra); NO external source.
520	  % LANDED axiom-clean as `AlgebraicGeometry.isIntegral_of_retract`, stated generically for any
521	  % retract `r : S ⟶ T`, `pr : T ⟶ S` with `r ≫ pr = 𝟙 S` of an integral `T` (more general than
522	  % the `X`/`X×Y`/`p_1` instance below). PROOF DIVERGENCE (cosmetic, sound either way): the Lean
523	  % proves reducedness PER-STALK (each `S.stalk x` embeds via `pr.stalkMap` into the reduced
524	  % `T.stalk`, then `isReduced_of_isReduced_stalk`), not via the global-sections split-injection
525	  % the prose below describes. Plan/blueprint-writer may align the prose to the stalk route.
526	  Let \(X \times Y\) be an integral scheme (here it is geometrically irreducible and reduced over
527	  \(\bar k\)). Suppose \(X\) is a \emph{retract} of \(X \times Y\): there is a section
528	  \(s \colon X \to X \times Y\) of the first projection \(p_1\), i.e.\ \(s \fatsemi p_1 = \mathbf 1_X\).
529	  Then \(X\) is integral.
530	\end{lemma}
531	\begin{proof}
532	\leanok
533	  Integrality is reducedness together with irreducibility, and the retract supplies each.
534	  \emph{Reduced.} The section identity \(s \fatsemi p_1 = \mathbf 1_X\) makes the pullback on global
535	  sections \(p_1^\sharp \colon \mathcal O_X(X) \to \mathcal O_{X \times Y}(X \times Y)\) split
536	  injective (it is left-inverted by \(s^\sharp\)). A split-injective ring map into the reduced ring
537	  \(\mathcal O_{X \times Y}(X \times Y)\) embeds \(\mathcal O_X(X)\) into a reduced ring, so it too is
538	  reduced (\(\mathtt{isReduced\_of\_injective}\) on the split injection); hence \(X\) is reduced.
539	  \emph{Irreducible.} The first projection \(p_1 \colon X \times Y \to X\) is surjective because it
540	  admits the section \(s\) (every point of \(X\) is \(p_1(s(\cdot))\)). The continuous image of an
541	  irreducible space under a surjection is irreducible; as \(X \times Y\) is irreducible and \(p_1\) is a
542	  continuous surjection onto \(X\), the space of \(X\) is irreducible.
543	  Reduced and irreducible together give that \(X\) is integral. This is elementary --- no cohomology,
544	  no external citation --- and feeds the Step-1 geometric assembly below, where the proper slice
545	  \(X_y \cong X\) must be presented as proper \emph{integral}.
546	\end{proof}
547	\begin{lemma}\leanok
548	[Step~1: per-closed-slice constancy of a proper slice into an affine]
549	  \label{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}
550	  
551	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}
552	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
553	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "For each \(y \in V\), the complete variety \(X \times \{y\}\) gets mapped by
554	  % \(f\) into the affine variety \(U\), and hence to a single point of \(U\). But this means that for
555	  % any \(x \in X\), \(y \in V\), \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)\), and this proves our
556	  % assertion." [Mumford, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma, p.~43.]
557	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43 (the
558	  ``for each \(y \in V\), the complete slice maps into the affine, hence to a single point'' step).}
559	  With the data of \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} --- \(\bar k\) algebraically
560	  closed, \(X\) proper over \(\bar k\), \(X \times Y\) geometrically irreducible, reduced, and locally of
561	  finite type, \(Z\) separated, a \(\bar k\)-point \(x_0 \colon \mathbf 1 \to X\), a morphism
562	  \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\), a \(p_2\)-saturated open \(U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})\) mapping into an
563	  affine open \(U_0 \subseteq Z\) --- fix a \emph{closed} point \(x\) of \(U\). Then \(f\) and the collapsed
564	  map \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) (with $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,
565	  (\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,(p_2)\(, i.e.\ \)(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)\() agree at \)x$
566	  after the residue-field probe \(\Spec \kappa(x) \to U\):
567	  \[ U.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi (U.\iota \fatsemi f)
568	     = U.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi (U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)). \]
569	\end{lemma}
570	\begin{proof}
571	  \leanok
572	  This is Mumford's per-slice step, realised \emph{cohomology-free} (route~B): the relative Stein /
573	  proper-pushforward \(f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O\) framing is a confirmed Mathlib gap and is
574	  deliberately avoided. The closed point \(x\) of \(U\) lies over a closed point
575	  \(y = p_2(x) \in V_{\mathrm{set}}\); since \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed and \(Y\) is of finite type
576	  over \(\bar k\), the residue field is \(\kappa(y) = \bar k\). Saturation \(U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})\)
577	  puts the \emph{entire} proper integral fibre \(X_y \cong X\) over \(y\) inside \(U\), so \(f\) maps \(X_y\)
578	  into the affine \(U_0\). Because \(X_y\) is proper integral over \(\bar k\), its global sections form a
579	  field (\(\mathtt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}\)), module-finite over \(\bar k\)
580	  (\(\mathtt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}\)); algebraic closedness collapses the finite

1	# Analogy: Mathlib path for `r_1.appTop isLocElem = 1` in Lane E
2	
3	## Mode
4	api-alignment
5	
6	## Slug
7	lane-e-projappiso
8	
9	## Iteration
10	189
11	
12	## Question
13	
14	Does Mathlib at b80f227 expose `(Proj.awayι _).appIso ⊤` as a usable
15	identity, and if not, what is the cleanest Mathlib-aligned recipe for
16	computing `r_1.appTop ((ΓSpecIso _).inv isLocElem) = (ΓSpecIso k̄).inv 1`
17	where `r_1 ≫ Proj.awayι (X 1) = onePt.left` with `onePt.left =
18	Proj.fromOfGlobalSections 𝒜 (evalIntoGlobal v=(1,1)) hφ`?
19	
20	## Project artifact(s)
21	
22	- `AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:106-163` — `iotaGm_onePt_chart1_factor` (constructs `r_1` via `IsOpenImmersion.lift` but packs it in an `∃`).
23	- `AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:252-439` — `iotaGm_chart1_composition_isOpenImmersion` (the blocker; ends with a residual `r_1.appTop(isLocElem) = 1` sorry at L439).
24	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/Points.lean:51-128` — `evalIntoGlobal`, `pointOfVec`, `onePt = pointOfVec (fun _ => 1) 0`.
25	
26	## Decisions identified
27	
28	### Decision: `IsOpenImmersion.lift_app` vs. `appTop`-of-composition for evaluating `r_1.appTop`
29	
30	- **Mathlib idiom**: `IsOpenImmersion.lift_app` at
31	  `Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/OpenImmersion.lean:696`:
32	  ```
33	  (lift f g H).app V = (f.appIso V).inv ≫ g.app (f ''ᵁ V) ≫
34	      X.presheaf.map (eqToHom (app_eq_invApp_app_of_comp_eq_aux _ _ _ (lift_fac ..).symm V).op
35	  ```
36	  Combined with `lift_uniq` (line 643), this is the canonical idiom for
37	  evaluating `r_1.app U` whenever `r_1 ≫ f = g` for an open immersion
38	  `f`. The `appIso` direction (`Γ(Y, f ''ᵁ U) ≅ Γ(X, U)`, line 190) is
39	  the right thing to use for the inverse "lift" direction; the `appTop`
40	  direction goes the wrong way (image-mismatch on degree-0 part, as the
41	  directive observed).
42	- **Project's current path**: iter-186 documented a 6-step recipe using
43	  `cancel_mono` to derive `r_1 = lift _ _ h_range` from `h_r_1`. This is
44	  structurally correct. Iter-188 attempted it but ran into reconstruction
45	  of `h_range` (already proven in `iotaGm_onePt_chart1_factor`, but
46	  unavailable because `r_1` is hidden inside an `∃`).
47	- **Gap**: divergent-with-cost. The `∃`-packaging of `r_1` forces the
48	  consumer to re-derive `h_range` via `cancel_mono`. Refactoring
49	  `iotaGm_onePt_chart1_factor` to expose `r_1` as a `noncomputable def`
50	  (with the lift body inline) and `h_r_1`, `h_range` as separate lemmas
51	  removes the re-derivation cost.
52	- **Cost of divergence**: ~30-50 LOC of `cancel_mono` plumbing in every
53	  consumer (currently just one: `iotaGm_chart1_composition_isOpenImmersion`),
54	  plus the structural confusion (iter-186 to iter-188, 3 iters lost to
55	  recipe rediscovery).
56	- **Verdict**: ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB — refactor `iotaGm_onePt_chart1_factor`
57	  to a `def` + lemma pair.
58	
59	### Decision: `(Proj.awayι _).appIso ⊤` direct simp lemma vs. manual chain
60	
61	- **Mathlib idiom**: There is NO direct simp lemma identifying
62	  `(Proj.awayι _).appIso ⊤` with `basicOpenIsoAway.symm ≪≫ (topIso etc.)`
63	  in Mathlib at b80f227. The available identifications:
64	  - `Proj.basicOpenIsoAway` (`Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/ProjectiveSpectrum/Basic.lean:179`):
65	    `Away 𝒜 f ≅ Γ(Proj 𝒜, basicOpen 𝒜 f)` via `awayToSection`.
66	  - `Proj.basicOpenToSpec_app_top` (line 143):
67	    `(basicOpenToSpec 𝒜 f).app ⊤ = (ΓSpecIso _).hom ≫ awayToSection ≫ basicOpen.topIso.inv`.
68	  - `Proj.opensRange_awayι` (line 199):
69	    `(Proj.awayι _).opensRange = Proj.basicOpen 𝒜 f`.
70	
71	  The consumer must wire these together: `awayι = basicOpenIsoSpec.inv ≫ basicOpen.ι`
72	  (line 190) ⟹ `awayι.app U = basicOpen.ι.app U ≫ basicOpenIsoSpec.inv.app (basicOpen.ι ⁻¹ᵁ U)`;
73	  for `U = basicOpen 𝒜 f`, this collapses via `basicOpenToSpec_app_top`.
74	- **Project's current path**: iter-188 attempted `simp` default and got
75	  stuck. No explicit manual wiring through `basicOpenIsoAway` was tried.
76	- **Gap**: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL (partial). The connecting lemma
77	  ```
78	  (Proj.awayι 𝒜 f f_deg hm).app (Proj.basicOpen 𝒜 f) ≫ basicOpenIsoAway.hom
79	    = (ΓSpecIso _).inv ≫ ... (topIso shuffle) ...
80	  ```
81	  is implicit in `basicOpenToSpec_app_top` + the definition of `awayι`,
82	  but no single simp lemma packages it. A 5-10 LOC Mathlib PR adding
83	  `Proj.awayι_app_basicOpen` or `Proj.awayι_appIso_top` would close the gap.
84	- **Cost of divergence**: ~30-40 LOC of manual `basicOpenIsoSpec.inv`
85	  unwinding per consumer. For just one consumer (this file), absorbable
86	  inline; if more Proj-of-graded-ring computations land downstream
87	  (Picard / etc.), upstream the PR.
88	- **Verdict**: PROCEED (project-side helper) + flag for Mathlib PR.
89	
90	### Decision: Compute `r_1.appTop` via `lift_app` vs. via `morphismRestrict`
91	
92	- **Mathlib idiom**: For `r_1 = lift (awayι 𝒜 (X 1)) onePt.left h_range`,
93	  the `lift_app` direction is more directly tied to the open-immersion
94	  universal property. The `morphismRestrict` direction (via
95	  `fromOfGlobalSections_morphismRestrict` line 493 +
96	  `fromOfGlobalSections_resLE` line 502) is more directly tied to the
97	  specific shape of `onePt.left = fromOfGlobalSections`.
98	  Both reach the same answer; `morphismRestrict` may be cleaner here
99	  because `fromOfGlobalSections_morphismRestrict` already says:
100	  ```
101	  (fromOfGlobalSections 𝒜 f hf) ∣_ (basicOpen 𝒜 r) =
102	    (Scheme.isoOfEq _).hom ≫ toBasicOpenOfGlobalSections 𝒜 f rfl hn hr
103	  ```
104	  This identifies the restriction of `onePt.left` to `basicOpen 𝒜 (X 1)`
105	  with the explicit `toBasicOpenOfGlobalSections` morphism — whose
106	  underlying ring map is `IsLocalization.map _ (evalIntoGlobal v)`.
107	- **Project's current path**: iter-186 recipe is via `lift_app`.
108	- **Gap**: divergent-equivalent (both work; `morphismRestrict` may be
109	  ~10-15 LOC shorter).
110	- **Verdict**: DIVERGE_INTENTIONALLY if the project prefers the `lift_app`
111	  recipe (it's a cleaner conceptual statement), but mention
112	  `morphismRestrict` as the structural alternative.
113	
114	## Recommendation
115	
116	**Primary path (recommended)**: refactor `iotaGm_onePt_chart1_factor` to
117	expose `r_1` as a `noncomputable def` plus a separate `lift_fac`-style
118	lemma. The current `∃`-packaging hides the `IsOpenImmersion.lift`
119	internals that the consumer needs. Once exposed, the iter-188 6-step
120	recipe collapses to:
121	
122	1. `unfold r_1_def` (now exposed) ⟹ `r_1 := IsOpenImmersion.lift (awayι _) onePt.left h_range`.
123	2. `rw [IsOpenImmersion.lift_app]` at `V = ⊤` ⟹ `appIso.inv ≫ onePt.left.app (basicOpen 𝒜 (X 1)) ≫ presheaf.map`.
124	3. Use `Proj.opensRange_awayι` to rewrite `awayι ''ᵁ ⊤ = basicOpen 𝒜 (X 1)`.
125	4. For `appIso ⊤ .inv (isLocElem)`: use `awayι = basicOpenIsoSpec.inv ≫ basicOpen.ι`, then `basicOpenToSpec_app_top` + `basicOpenIsoAway.hom = awayToSection` to identify the image as `basicOpenIsoAway.hom isLocElem ∈ Γ(Proj 𝒜, basicOpen)`.
126	5. Use `fromOfGlobalSections_morphismRestrict` (or `_resLE`) on `onePt.left` restricted to `basicOpen 𝒜 (X 1)` ⟹ `toBasicOpenOfGlobalSections`-shape morphism whose ring map is `IsLocalization.map _ (evalIntoGlobal (1,1))`.
127	6. Evaluate on `isLocElem = X_0 / X_1`: `IsLocalization.map _ (evalIntoGlobal v) sends [X_0 / X_1] ↦ (evalIntoGlobal v X_0) / (evalIntoGlobal v X_1) = 1 / 1 = 1` (in `Localization.Away 1 = k̄`).
128	
129	Realistic budget: **~60-80 LOC**, not 30-50. The directive's 30-50 LOC
130	target underestimates the appIso plumbing.
131	
132	**Secondary observation**: if the project envisions more
133	`Proj.fromOfGlobalSections` ↔ chart calculations downstream (e.g. in
134	Picard / FGA layer), a small Mathlib PR adding a direct
135	`Proj.awayι_appIso_top` simp lemma (5-10 LOC, derived from
136	`basicOpenToSpec_app_top`) would generalize this work and remove the
137	chase across all consumers. Not blocking iter-190.

{"file_path":"/home/archon/FormalizationProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge/.lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Over.lean","content":"/-
Copyright (c) 2024 Andrew Yang. All rights reserved.
Released under Apache 2.0 license as described in the file LICENSE.
Authors: Andrew Yang
-/
module

public import Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme
public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Comma.Over.OverClass

/-!
# Typeclasses for `S`-schemes and `S`-morphisms

We define these as thin wrappers around `CategoryTheory/Comma/OverClass`.

## Main definition
- `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over`: `X.Over S` equips `X` with an `S`-scheme structure.
  `X ↘ S : X ⟶ S` is the structure morphism.
- `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.IsOver`: `f.IsOver S` asserts that `f` is an `S`-morphism.

-/

@[expose] public section

namespace AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme

universe u

open CategoryTheory

variable {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : X.Hom Y) (S S' : Scheme.{u})

/--
`X.Over S` is the typeclass containing the data of a structure morphism `X ↘ S : X ⟶ S`.
-/
protected abbrev Over (X S : Scheme.{u}) := OverClass X S

/--
`X.CanonicallyOver S` is the typeclass containing the data of a structure morphism `X ↘ S : X ⟶ S`,
and that `S` is (uniquely) inferable from the structure of `X`.
-/
abbrev CanonicallyOver (X S : Scheme.{u}) := CanonicallyOverClass X S

/-- Given `X.Over S` and `Y.Over S` and `f : X ⟶ Y`,
`f.IsOver S` is the typeclass asserting `f` commutes with the structure morphisms. -/
abbrev Hom.IsOver (f : X.Hom Y) (S : Scheme.{u}) [X.Over S] [Y.Over S] := HomIsOver f S

@[simp]
lemma Hom.isOver_iff [X.Over S] [Y.Over S] {f : X ⟶ Y} : f.IsOver S ↔ f ≫ Y ↘ S = X ↘ S :=
  ⟨fun H ↦ H.1, fun h ↦ ⟨h⟩⟩

/-! Also note the existence of `CategoryTheory.IsOverTower X Y S`. -/

/-- Given `X.Over S`, this is the bundled object of `Over S`. -/
abbrev asOver (X S : Scheme.{u}) [X.Over S] := OverClass.asOver X S

/-- Given a morphism `X ⟶ Y` with `f.IsOver S`, this is the bundled morphism in `Over S`. -/
abbrev Hom.asOver (f : X.Hom Y) (S : Scheme.{u}) [X.Over S] [Y.Over S] [f.IsOver S] :=
  OverClass.asOverHom S f

end AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme
"}

input.new_string:
-- Iter-190 prover closure (point-witness reshape). The iter-184 closed body relied on
  -- `rw [← Scheme.Hom.coe_opensRange]` to expose the RHS `Set.range` as a `(...).opensRange`
  -- coercion; in the standalone signature here, elaboration types the LHS at
  -- `Set ↥(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left` whereas `Proj.awayι _ _ _ _` is typed at
  -- `Set ↥(Proj 𝒜)`, so the rewrite pattern does not match. We sidestep this by
  -- destructuring point-wise FIRST, then `change`-ing the membership goal into
  -- `(...).opensRange.1` (a defeq reshape that works because the underlying carriers
  -- `↥(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left` and `↥(Proj 𝒜)` are defeq through
  -- `Scheme.asOver` / `OverClass.asOver`). The remainder of the chain is identical to
  -- the iter-184 body.
  rintro _ ⟨x, rfl⟩
  change (ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar).left.base x ∈ (Proj.awayι _ _ _ _).opensRange.1
  rw [Proj.opensRange_awayι]
  change x ∈ (ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar).left ⁻¹ᵁ Proj.basicOpen _ _
  change x ∈ Proj.fromOfGlobalSections (projectiveLineBarGrading kbar) _ _ ⁻¹ᵁ _
  rw [Proj.fromOfGlobalSections_preimage_basicOpen _ _ _
      (projectiveLineBarAffineCover_hm 1)
      (projectiveLineBarAffineCover_fDeg kbar 1)]
  refine (Scheme.basicOpen_of_isUnit _ ?_).symm ▸ TopologicalSpace.Opens.mem_top x
  change IsUnit ((Scheme.ΓSpecIso (CommRingCat.of kbar)).inv.hom
    ((MvPolynomial.eval (fun _ : Fin 2 => (1 : kbar)))
      ((![MvPolynomial.X 0, MvPolynomial.X 1] :
        Fin 2 → MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar) 1)))
  simp

211:    f.app U ≫ (f.appIso (f ⁻¹ᵁ U)).inv =
218:    f.app U ≫ (f.appIso (f ⁻¹ᵁ U)).inv =
225:    (f.appIso U).inv ≫ f.app (f ''ᵁ U) = X.presheaf.map (eqToHom (preimage_image_eq f U)).op :=
231:    f.appLE U V e ≫ (f.appIso V).inv =
242:    (f.appIso U).inv ≫ f.appLE (f ''ᵁ U) V e =
249:    (f.appIso U).inv ≫ f.app _ ≫

321	/-- Given a family of homogeneous elements `f` of positive degree that spans the irrelevant ideal,
322	`Spec (A_f)₀ ⟶ Proj A` forms an affine open cover of `Proj A`. -/
323	noncomputable
324	def affineOpenCoverOfIrrelevantLESpan {ι : Type*} (f : ι → A) {m : ι → ℕ}
325	    (f_deg : ∀ i, f i ∈ 𝒜 (m i)) (hm : ∀ i, 0 < m i)
326	    (hf : (HomogeneousIdeal.irrelevant 𝒜).toIdeal ≤ Ideal.span (Set.range f)) :
327	    (Proj 𝒜).AffineOpenCover where
328	  I₀ := ι
329	  X i := .of (Away 𝒜 (f i))
330	  f i := awayι 𝒜 (f i) (f_deg i) (hm i)
331	  idx x := (mem_iSup.mp ((iSup_basicOpen_eq_top 𝒜 f hf).ge (Set.mem_univ x))).choose
332	  covers x := by
333	    change x ∈ (awayι 𝒜 _ _ _).opensRange
334	    rw [opensRange_awayι]
335	    exact (mem_iSup.mp ((iSup_basicOpen_eq_top 𝒜 f hf).ge (Set.mem_univ x))).choose_spec

337	/-- `Proj A` is covered by `Spec (A_f)₀` for all homogeneous elements of positive degree. -/
338	@[simps! f] noncomputable
339	def affineOpenCover : (Proj 𝒜).AffineOpenCover :=
340	  affineOpenCoverOfIrrelevantLESpan 𝒜
341	    (ι := Σ i : PNat, 𝒜 i) (m := fun i ↦ i.1) (fun i ↦ i.2) (fun i ↦ i.2.2) (fun i ↦ i.1.2) <| by
342	  classical
343	  intro z hz
344	  rw [← DirectSum.sum_support_decompose 𝒜 z]
345	  refine Ideal.sum_mem _ fun c hc ↦ if hc0 : c = 0 then ?_ else
346	    Ideal.subset_span ⟨⟨⟨c, Nat.pos_iff_ne_zero.mpr hc0⟩, _⟩, rfl⟩
347	  convert Ideal.zero_mem _
348	  subst hc0
349	  exact hz

873	variable [AddSubgroupClass σ A] {𝒜 : ℕ → σ} [GradedRing 𝒜]
874	variable {e d : ℕ} {f : A} (hf : f ∈ 𝒜 d) {g : A} (hg : g ∈ 𝒜 e)
875	
876	/-- The element `t := g ^ d / f ^ e` such that `A_{(fg)} = A_{(f)}[1/t]`. -/
877	abbrev Away.isLocalizationElem : Away 𝒜 f :=
878	  Away.mk 𝒜 hf e (g ^ d) (by convert SetLike.pow_mem_graded d hg using 2; exact mul_comm _ _)
879	
880	variable {x : A} (hx : x = f * g)
881	
882	/-- Let `t := g ^ d / f ^ e`, then `A_{(fg)} = A_{(f)}[1/t]`. -/
883	theorem Away.isLocalization_mul (hd : d ≠ 0) :
884	    letI := (awayMap 𝒜 hg hx).toAlgebra
885	    IsLocalization.Away (isLocalizationElem hf hg) (Away 𝒜 x) := by
886	  letI := (awayMap 𝒜 hg hx).toAlgebra
887	  constructor; constructor
888	  · rintro ⟨r, n, rfl⟩
889	    rw [map_pow, RingHom.algebraMap_toAlgebra]
890	    let z : Away 𝒜 x := Away.mk 𝒜 (hx ▸ SetLike.mul_mem_graded hf hg) (d + e)
891	        (g ^ e * f ^ (2 * e + d)) <| by
892	      convert SetLike.mul_mem_graded (SetLike.pow_mem_graded e hg)
893	        (SetLike.pow_mem_graded (2 * e + d) hf) using 2
894	      ring
895	    refine (isUnit_iff_exists_inv.mpr ⟨z, ?_⟩).pow _
896	    ext
897	    simp only [val_mul, val_one, awayMap_mk, Away.val_mk, z, Localization.mk_mul]
898	    rw [← Localization.mk_one, Localization.mk_eq_mk_iff, Localization.r_iff_exists]
899	    use 1
900	    simp only [OneMemClass.coe_one, one_mul, Submonoid.coe_mul, mul_one, hx]
901	    ring
902	  · intro z
903	    obtain ⟨n, s, hs, rfl⟩ := Away.mk_surjective 𝒜 (hx ▸ SetLike.mul_mem_graded hf hg) z
904	    rcases d with - | d

`iotaGm_chart1_composition_isOpenImmersion` can apply `IsOpenImmersion.lift_app`
directly to `iotaGm_r_1` instead of re-deriving the lift identity via `cancel_mono`. -/

# AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean

## iter-190 prover summary

**Net: 3 → 2 sorries (−1, axiom-clean closure on sub-task b).**

Entering iter-190 prover (after plan-phase Lane E refactor):
- L104 `iotaGm_r_1_range_subset` — sub-task (b) range containment (NEW iter-190)
- L277 `iotaGm_chart1_composition_isOpenImmersion` — sub-task (f) composition is open immersion (Stage 8 residual)
- L788 `genusZero_curve_iso_P1` — RR bridge (untouched)

Exiting iter-190 prover:
- L104 `iotaGm_r_1_range_subset` — **CLOSED axiom-clean** (verified `#axioms = {propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}`)
- L261 `iotaGm_chart1_composition_isOpenImmersion` — untouched (no progress on the appTop telescope residual)
- L766 `genusZero_curve_iso_P1` — untouched

## iotaGm_r_1_range_subset (L104) — RESOLVED axiom-clean

### Attempt 1
- **Approach:** Reuse the iter-184 closed proof body directly. The original closure was
  ```
  rw [← Scheme.Hom.coe_opensRange (Proj.awayι _ _ _ _), Proj.opensRange_awayι]
  rintro _ ⟨x, rfl⟩
  rw [SetLike.mem_coe, ← Scheme.Hom.mem_preimage]
  change x ∈ Proj.fromOfGlobalSections _ _ _ ⁻¹ᵁ Proj.basicOpen ...
  ...
  ```
- **Result:** FAILED — `rw [← Scheme.Hom.coe_opensRange (Proj.awayι _ _ _ _)]` did not unify
  the `Set.range ⇑(Proj.awayι ...)` pattern (typed at `Set ↥(Proj 𝒜)`) against the goal RHS
  (typed at `Set ↥(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left`). `rw` does not unfold the `.left`/`asOver`
  projection.
- **Dead end:** Direct iter-184 body transplant.

### Attempt 2 — RESOLVED
- **Approach:** Reshape the membership step-by-step via `change` instead of trying to
  rewrite `Set.range`. The chain:
  1. `rintro _ ⟨x, rfl⟩` to destructure the range subset.
  2. `change (ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar).left.base x ∈ (Proj.awayι _ _ _ _).opensRange.1`
     — defeq reshape from `Set.range` to `.opensRange.1`.
  3. `rw [Proj.opensRange_awayι]` — now rewrites the `.opensRange` cleanly to
     `Proj.basicOpen 𝒜 (X 1)`.
  4. `change x ∈ (ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar).left ⁻¹ᵁ Proj.basicOpen _ _` — flip
     to a preimage membership statement.
  5. `change x ∈ Proj.fromOfGlobalSections _ _ _ ⁻¹ᵁ _` — unfold `onePt.left` to its
     `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections` shape (the `pointOfVec` projection is defeq through
     `Over.homMk`).
  6. `rw [Proj.fromOfGlobalSections_preimage_basicOpen ...]` — reduces to a
     `(Spec k̄).basicOpen` membership on the evaluation `evalIntoGlobal v (X 1)`.
  7. `refine (Scheme.basicOpen_of_isUnit _ ?_).symm ▸ Opens.mem_top x` — basic open of
     a unit is `⊤`.
  8. Final unit check: `IsUnit ((ΓSpecIso _).inv.hom (eval (1,1) (X 1)))` reduces to
     `IsUnit 1` by `simp` (via `eval_X` + `map_one`).
- **Result:** RESOLVED. `#axioms iotaGm_r_1_range_subset = {propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}`.
- **Key insight:** The carrier-type mismatch between `↥(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left`
  and `↥(Proj (projectiveLineBarGrading kbar))` is bridgeable by `change` (defeq through
  `Scheme.asOver` / `OverClass.asOver`) but NOT by `rw`. The fix is structural: rephrase
  the membership goal via `(...).opensRange.1` reshape BEFORE invoking
  `Proj.opensRange_awayι`. This makes the propagated `iotaGm_r_1` def and `iotaGm_r_1_fac`
  lemma axiom-clean as well.

### Verification
- `lean_verify AlgebraicGeometry.iotaGm_r_1_range_subset` → `{propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}` ✓
- `lean_verify AlgebraicGeometry.iotaGm_r_1_fac` → `{propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}` ✓

## iotaGm_chart1_composition_isOpenImmersion (L261) — NOT CLOSED

### Attempt 1
- **Approach:** Per PROGRESS.md objective (2), close the Stage 8 residual `appTop`
  equation by building the auxiliary helper
  `r_1_appTop_isLocElem_eq_one : r_1.appTop ((ΓSpecIso _).inv isLocElem) = (ΓSpecIso _).inv 1`
  per `analogies/lane-e-projappiso.md` (cancel_mono uniqueness of `r_1 = IsOpenImmersion.lift _ _ _`
  + `IsOpenImmersion.lift_app` + `Proj.basicOpenIsoAway` + `fromOfGlobalSections_morphismRestrict`
  + `IsLocalization.map` on the chart-1 affine coord). Then plug the helper into the Stage 8
  residual after further `simp`/`ext` telescoping.
- **Result:** NOT ATTEMPTED with full body — scope assessment showed the helper alone
  requires ~60–80 LOC (per analogist) and the Stage 8 residual needs an additional
  ~40–60 LOC of `pullback.lift_*`/`pullbackSpecIso_*`/`pullbackRightPullbackFstIso_*`
  simp lemma chases at the `.app ⊤` level (not packaged as named lemmas in Mathlib).
  Total realistic budget ~150 LOC, exceeding iter-190 prover phase budget after
  closing sub-task (b).
- **Negative search:** `lean_local_search pullback.lift_fst_app|pullback.lift_snd_app|pullback.congrHom_hom_app`
  — Mathlib has none of these as named simp lemmas at b80f227. Each consumer must unfold
  `pullback.lift.app` via `Scheme.Hom.comp_app` + `Scheme.Hom.app` on the underlying
  morphism, which is exactly the brittle "iter-186 raw appTop chain" form documented
  in the comments at L399–447.
- **Next step (iter-191+):** Either
  (a) Build the helper `r_1_appTop_isLocElem_eq_one` as a typed pin (~60-80 LOC sketch
      in the analogist file `lane-e-projappiso.md`), keep the big body as `sorry`
      consuming the pin (net +1 then -1 once the pin closes). Helper budget +1.
  (b) Refactor `iotaGm_chart1_composition_isOpenImmersion` to consume `iotaGm_r_1` and
      `iotaGm_r_1_fac` directly (drop the abstract `r_1, h_r_1` parameters), then the
      `appIso ⊤ .inv` action on `isLocElem` collapses through `IsOpenImmersion.lift_app`
      directly on the `iotaGm_r_1` body. Estimated ~30 LOC for the refactor + ~80 LOC
      for the full closure.

Approach (b) is preferable because the abstract parameter form is leftover from the
iter-189 refactor of `iotaGm_onePt_chart1_factor` and is no longer needed: the only
consumer (`iotaGm_isOpenImmersion`) supplies `iotaGm_r_1` and `iotaGm_r_1_fac` verbatim,
so specialization is free.

## genusZero_curve_iso_P1 (L766) — UNCHANGED

RR bridge (Lane H). Off-limits iter-190 per PROGRESS.md "Off-limits / deferred" and the
H1Vanishing chapter scaffolding deferred to iter-191+.

## Discoveries for iter-191+ plan-phase

- The `Scheme.asOver` / `OverClass.asOver` carrier identification `↥(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left ≡ ↥(Proj 𝒜)`
  is defeq-bridgeable by `change` but NOT `rw`. Any future Lane E body relying on
  `Proj.opensRange_awayι` or `Proj.basicOpenIsoAway` must reshape via `change` before
  rewriting.
- Recommended refactor for the iter-191+ Lane E closure: drop `r_1, h_r_1` abstract
  parameters from `iotaGm_chart1_composition_isOpenImmersion` (and downstream
  `iotaGm_chart1_section`) and specialise on `iotaGm_r_1` / `iotaGm_r_1_fac` directly.
  This makes `IsOpenImmersion.lift_app` apply by `unfold iotaGm_r_1` without needing
  the `cancel_mono` uniqueness step. ~30 LOC structural refactor + ~80 LOC closure
  (per analogist's recipe) — total ~110 LOC over 1–2 iters.

## Blueprint markers (for review agent)

- `iotaGm_r_1_range_subset` is axiom-clean (no `sorry`); the iter-184 dependent
  `iotaGm_r_1` def and `iotaGm_r_1_fac` lemma are also axiom-clean (sync_leanok will
  add `\leanok` to any corresponding statement/proof blocks).
- No new blueprint blocks added; no `\leanok` markers to flag.

content:
1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
152

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128	
129	Before stopping:
130	
131	1. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with: current result, lemmas discovered, concrete next step, dead-end warnings.
132	2. Save all changes; ensure the file compiles (use scoped `sorry` for stuck parts if needed).

9	# Auslander–Buchsbaum formula (A.4.b)
10	
11	This file is the **A.4.b** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the project's
12	positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It packages the
13	Auslander–Buchsbaum formula and the corollary "regular local ring
14	⟹ Cohen–Macaulay" that the sibling sub-build **A.4.a** (codim-1 extension of a
15	rational map across a codim-2 closed point on a regular projective surface)
16	consumes.
17	
18	Per STRATEGY.md L30 this row is gated downstream on A.4.a but is independently
19	startable on the Mathlib side: the algebra here is decoupled from the Albanese
20	geometry and lives entirely under `RingTheory.*`. Mathlib at the project's
21	pinned commit (`b80f227`) exposes `IsRegularLocalRing` and the categorical
22	`CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension` on an abelian category (specialised to
23	`ModuleCat R`), as well as the regular-sequence definition
24	`RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular`. The depth function `Module.depth`, the
25	Auslander–Buchsbaum formula, and the Cohen–Macaulay predicate are *not* in
26	Mathlib at the pinned commit — they are the new content scaffolded here.
